metadata.source: King Fahd Complex for the Printing of the Holy Quran

surahs.significance: Foundation of Islamic prayer, recited in every rak'ah of the five daily prayers

otherNames: Fustaat al-Quran (The Tent of the Quran)
Sinam al-Quran (The Peak of the Quran)
surahs.significance: Longest surah, contains many fundamental Islamic laws and stories
Contains stories of Jesus and Mary, emphasizes unity of believers
Major source of Islamic law regarding women, marriage, and inheritance
Last revealed surah, contains final Islamic laws and regulations
Emphasizes monotheism and refutes idolatry, revealed in one sitting
Contains detailed stories of earlier prophets and their communities
Discusses rules of warfare and the Battle of Badr
Only surah without Bismillah, deals with treaties and hypocrites
Named after Prophet Jonah, emphasizes divine mercy and guidance
Stories of Prophet Hud and other messengers, warnings to disbelievers
Complete story of Prophet Joseph in one surah, called 'Ahsan al-Qasas'
Emphasizes divine signs in nature and the truth of revelation
Highlights Prophet Abraham's mission and divine guidance
Named after the people of Al-Hijr (Thamud), promises Quran's protection
Emphasizes divine blessings and creation, mentions bees as divine sign
Mentions Prophet's night journey, contains fundamental moral teachings
Contains four famous stories, recommended for Friday recitation
Named after Mary, mother of Jesus, emphasizes prophetic lineage
Detailed story of Moses and Pharaoh, begins with mysterious letters
Mentions many prophets and their missions, emphasizes unity of message
Details hajj pilgrimage rites and their spiritual significance
Begins with qualities of successful believers, emphasizes afterlife
Contains social laws, modesty guidelines, and the famous Light Verse
Describes Quran as criterion between truth and falsehood
Stories of prophets and their opposition, ends with poets' description
Features story of Solomon and Queen of Sheba, mentions speaking ant
Detailed story of Moses from birth to prophethood
Compares false gods to spider's web, emphasizes trials of faith
Contains prophecy about Roman victory over Persians, signs of Allah in creation
Contains wise advice of Luqman to his son, emphasizes parental rights
Contains verse of prostration (sajdah), emphasizes creation and resurrection
Deals with Battle of the Trench, laws of marriage, adoption, and hijab
Stories of Prophet David and Solomon, Queen of Sheba, importance of gratitude
Emphasizes Allah as the Originator of creation, role of angels
Called 'Heart of the Quran', focuses on resurrection and afterlife
Stories of multiple prophets, emphasizes sacrifice of Abraham
Stories of Prophets David, Solomon, and Job, emphasizes divine wisdom
Emphasizes pure monotheism, divine mercy, and scenes of Judgment Day
Story of believer among Pharaoh's people, emphasizes Allah's forgiveness
Explains Quran's detailed guidance, contains verse of prostration
Emphasizes importance of consultation (shura) in decision-making
Warns against being deceived by worldly gold and ornaments
Mentions smoke as a sign before Day of Judgment, Night of Decree
Describes how all nations will kneel before Allah on Judgment Day
Story of 'Ad people in sand dunes, emphasizes kindness to parents
Only surah named after Prophet Muhammad, deals with believers and fighting
Revealed about Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, called a manifest victory
Contains important Islamic social etiquette and brotherhood principles
Emphasizes resurrection after death, recording angels, and afterlife
Swears by different types of winds, stories of Abraham and Lot
Swears by Mount Tur (Sinai), describes paradise and hell vividly
Describes Prophet's Night Journey (Miraj), contains verse of prostration
Mentions splitting of moon, stories of past nations and their destruction
Known as 'Bride of the Quran', repeatedly asks 'Which favors will you deny?'
Describes three groups of people on Judgment Day and their different fates
Emphasizes divine sovereignty and the importance of charity and faith
Addresses family disputes and emphasizes justice and proper conduct
Contains the beautiful names of Allah and discusses the exile of Banu Nadir
Addresses relationships with non-Muslims and loyalty to faith
Emphasizes unity in ranks and mentions Jesus's prophecy of Muhammad
Establishes the importance of Friday prayer and community worship
Exposes the nature and dangers of hypocrisy in the Muslim community
Discusses the Day of Judgment as a day of mutual loss and gain
Provides detailed guidance on divorce procedures and family law
Addresses the Prophet's family matters and provides examples of faith
Protects from grave punishment, emphasizes Allah's absolute sovereignty
Defends the Prophet's character and emphasizes the importance of knowledge
Describes the inevitable Day of Judgment and validates the Quran
Describes the ascension to Allah and human characteristics
Narrates Noah's persistent call to his people and their destruction
Describes the jinn's acceptance of Islam after hearing the Quran
Commands night prayer and gradual Quran recitation
Commands the Prophet to warn mankind and describes hell's guardians
Describes the Day of Resurrection and proves life after death
Describes human creation and the rewards of the righteous
Uses natural phenomena to describe the Day of Judgment
Addresses the great news of resurrection and judgment
Describes angels of death and the story of Moses and Pharaoh
Teaches about treating all people equally regardless of social status
Describes cosmic changes on the Day of Judgment
Describes the splitting of the sky on the Day of Judgment
Condemns cheating in business and describes the record books
Describes the splitting of heaven and receiving of records
Tells the story of the People of the Ditch and persecution of believers
Uses the morning star to affirm the truth of the Quran
Glorifies Allah and mentions the scriptures of Abraham and Moses
Describes the overwhelming nature of the Day of Judgment and contrasts paradise with hell
Opens with oath by dawn, tells of destroyed nations, and describes the peaceful soul
Refers to Mecca as the city and emphasizes the steep path of righteousness
Opens with oaths by cosmic phenomena, emphasizes soul purification and corruption
Contrasts night and day, emphasizes charity and piety leading to different paths
Comforts the Prophet, reminds of Allah's continuous favor and care
Describes Allah's favors to the Prophet including expanding his chest and removing his burden
Opens with oath by fig and olive, emphasizes human creation and righteousness
First revelation to Prophet Muhammad, emphasizes reading, knowledge, and creation
Describes the Night of Power when the Quran was first revealed
Addresses People of the Book and emphasizes pure monotheistic religion
Describes the final earthquake and emphasizes that all deeds will be weighed
Opens with oath by charging war horses, criticizes human ingratitude and materialism
Describes the Day of Judgment as a striking calamity with vivid imagery
Criticizes excessive competition for worldly gain and warns of the hereafter
Considered essence of the Quran, emphasizes faith, righteous deeds, truth, and patience
Condemns those who backbite and slander, warns of hell's punishment
Tells of Abraha's failed attack on Kaaba with elephants, year of Prophet's birth
References the trade journeys of Quraysh and calls them to worship Allah
Criticizes those who deny judgment, neglect orphans, and show off in worship
Shortest surah, promises Prophet abundance and tells him to pray and sacrifice
Establishes clear religious boundaries, 'to you your religion and to me mine'
Last complete surah revealed, about conquest of Mecca and people entering Islam
Condemns Abu Lahab and his wife for their opposition to Islam
Pure monotheism, equals one-third of Quran in reward when recited
First of two protective surahs (Al-Mu'awwidhatain), seeks refuge from evil
Final surah of Quran, second protective surah, seeks refuge from Satan's whispers
